subject: [PATCH v3] memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author downplayed a concern about potential memory leaks due to bounded nature of the issue and previous behavior of silently dropping sub-page charges.

Author acknowledged that the patch may have issues for long-running cgroups, but decided that adding complexity to fix this issue is not worth it.
reviews.raw_body:
This is not much a concern as it is bounded (i.e. nr_nodes * PAGE_SIZE - 1) and
previously for the warning case, we were already dropping the sub-page charges.

---

This can be a concern for a long running cgroup. However fixing this would add
complexity not worth it. This is a temporary fix and will be reverted in newer
kernels.

2026-05-18.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a regression introduced by reverting the patch, explaining that it reintroduces node LRU locking in the objcg reparenting path.

Author acknowledged a significant regression in the patch's performance and is considering alternative solutions, including backporting a multi-objcg patch to fix it.
reviews.raw_body:
The issue with that revert is that we reintroduce all node lru locking in the
objcg reparenting path.

---

I am waiting for Oliver to test the multi-objcg patch I sent. If that also
resolves the regression then we have one more option i.e. backport that to 7.1
to fix the regression.

So to summarize, for future kernels we will be having multi-objcg in some form.
For 7.1, we have to decide between (1) do nothing (2) this patch or (3) backport
the multi-objcg path to 7.1.

Andrew, please don't send this patch to Linus until we decide on the option.

2026-05-19.patch_summary: This patch modifies the way memory cgroup (memcg) caches object stock, switching from caching by object color group pointer to caching by memcg. This change aims to address a significant performance regression introduced in a previous version of the kernel, which resulted in a 67% slowdown on certain systems. The solution is expected to be fully implemented within one more development cycle.

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo expressed concern about the per-node object change introduced in v5, citing an unexpected last-minute regression and correctness concerns, and suggested reverting this change for v7.1 to ensure a safer option.
reviews.raw_body:
That is valid point, unfortunately.

One more thing I have to ask... for v7.1, wouldn't it be a safer option 
to revert the per-node object change and re-introduce it once we have a 
cleaner solution?

This change was introduced in v5, but the implementation before v4 had 
been exposed in -next for a while, and I think we don't have enough 
justification to keep the per-node objcgs change, at least for v7.1, 
given that we have an unexpected last-minute regression and
correctness concerns (albeit slight).

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary:
Reviewer questioned the severity of the all-node locking issue, suggesting it might not be a significant problem in practice, especially considering that critical section for reparenting is independent of folio count and large servers rarely go beyond 8 NUMA nodes.

Reviewer Harry Yoo expressed concern that the patch's solution might not be ideal if per-node objcg is required in future kernels, and noted a significant regression on 7.1.

Reviewer Harry Yoo expressed concern about the patch causing a significant regression, specifically mentioning a 67% increase in performance degradation on a specific kernel version (7.1), and suggested that the issue should not be ignored.
sentiment_signals: requested additional consideration of the all-node locking issue
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not sure the problems with all-node locking are serious enough to 
rule it out as an option for 7.1.

It is not ideal, but given that the critical section for reparenting is 
independent of folio count, would this actually be a significant problem 
in practice? (even large servers rarely go beyond 8 NUMA nodes...)

---

Yeah, If per-node objcg is required in future kernels anyway, this 
option would be more ideal (if available).

---

Ack.

Thanks!

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

2026-05-20.patch_summary: This patch modifies the way memory cgroup (memcg) caches object stock, switching from caching by object color group pointer to caching by memcg. This change aims to address a significant performance regression introduced in a previous version of the kernel, which resulted in a 67% slowdown on certain systems. The solution is expected to be fully implemented within one more development cycle.
